title: Course Project
text:
NoteEE656 Course Project



The project is a major component of the course. You will choose a topic in robotics perception, implement or validate a method, and communicate your results in a short technical report and presentation.

title: Course Policy
text:
NoteEE656 Course Policy



This page summarizes grading, collaboration rules, and course resources.
Updates will be reflected here throughout the semester.

title: Course Policy
text:
Grading






ImportantGrading scheme (overview)











Component
Weight
What it includes




Assignments
40%
Homework sets and programming/computation tasks


Quizzes
20%
Short quizzes (in-class or announced)


Project
40%
Proposal, checkpoint, final report, and presentation/demo











Note: Exact quiz count/timing and project milestone dates will be posted on the Schedule and Project pages.

title: Course Policy
text:
Academic integrity and collaboration






WarningWhat is allowed vs not allowed



✅ Allowed - Discussing concepts and approaches at a high level with classmates - Studying together and comparing understanding without sharing written solutions - Using course notes and posted examples
❌ Not allowed - Copying any part of another student’s written solution - Copying code, derivations, or calculations (even if you “understand it”) - Submitting work that is not fully your own


If you collaborate, your submitted work must be written independently and reflect your own understanding.

title: Course Policy
text:
Generative AI tools






Unless explicitly permitted by the instructor for a specific assignment, the use of generative AI tools for any part of your submitted work is not allowed and will be treated as plagiarism.

title: Course Policy
text:
Resources

Textbooks

[TBF] Probabilistic Robotics (Thrun, Burgard, Fox) Core foundations of probabilistic state estimation in robotics: Bayes filters, Kalman/particle filters, localization, mapping, and SLAM, with a strong emphasis on reasoning under uncertainty.
[BAR] State Estimation for Robotics (Barfoot) A modern, implementation-oriented treatment of estimation: least-squares, Kalman filtering, nonlinear estimation, Lie groups, and continuous-time trajectory estimation.
[CAK] SLAM Handbook: From Localization and Mapping to Spatial Intelligence (Carlone, Luca; Kim, Ayoung; Barfoot, Timothy; Cremers, Daniel; Dellaert, Frank) A broad handbook covering factor-graph SLAM foundations, sensing modalities (camera/LiDAR/radar/IMU), robust optimization, evaluation methodology, and emerging directions in spatial intelligence.
[LP] Modern Robotics: Mechanics, Planning, and Control (Lynch KM, Park FC), Cambridge University Press; 2017.



Software

MATLAB/Simulink and/or Python may be used for assignments and project work (details will be announced).







TipRecommendation



If you need a refresher on rigid-body transformations, Jacobians, and basic dynamics, start reviewing early.
The Schedule page will list what you need for each week.

title: Homework Policies
text:
NoteHomework Policies (EE656)



This page contains homework rules and procedures. For due dates and files, see the Homework page.

title: Homework Policies
text:
Submission guidelines






TipWhat to submit




Submit a single PDF unless the assignment explicitly requests additional files.
Your submission must be clear and readable (high-quality scan or direct PDF export).
If code is required, include it as instructed (either embedded in the PDF or as a separate upload).




Good practices

Start each question on a new page (recommended).
For multi-page answers, keep pages in order and label them clearly.
If using Gradescope, map pages to questions accurately.

title: Homework Policies
text:
Late day policy






You have 6 late days total for the semester, with a maximum of 2 late days per assignment.

Each late day is a 24-hour extension.
No credit is given after 48 hours past the deadline.
You are responsible for tracking your remaining late days.

title: Homework Policies
text:
Collaboration policy and honor code






WarningCollaboration rules



✅ Allowed - Discussing concepts and high-level approaches with classmates - Studying together and comparing understanding without sharing written solutions
❌ Not allowed - Copying any part of another student’s solution - Sharing or copying code, derivations, or calculations - Submitting work that is not fully your own


If you use any external resources (e.g., a textbook example, an online reference, or a small snippet of code), you must cite it clearly in your submission.

title: Homework Policies
text:
Generative AI tools policy






Unless explicitly permitted by the instructor for a specific homework, the use of generative AI tools for any part of your work is not allowed and will be treated as plagiarism.

title: Homework Policies
text:
Regrade policy
Regrade requests must be submitted via the course submission system (e.g., Gradescope) and must include a clear explanation.

Deadline: within 1 week of receiving your grade, by 11:59 PM.
We reserve the right to regrade the entire homework; your score may go up or down.


Legitimate regrade requests

Points were added incorrectly.
The grader missed your work because it was on a different page.
The grader claims your method fails in a specific case, but it works as implemented.



Illegitimate regrade requests

Disagreeing with the rubric without a grading error.
“My solution wasn’t clear, but what I meant is correct.”

title: Homework
text:
NoteSubmission



Homework is due by 11:59 PM (Asia/Riyadh) unless stated otherwise.
Submit via Gradescope. Your code should be given as a shared folder (e.g. Onedrive) or a Github repository.

title: Homework
text:
Policies


Collaboration policy and honor code
Homework must be completed individually.
You may discuss concepts and approaches at a high level, but your submitted work must be entirely your own.

Copying any part of another student’s solution is not allowed.
Copying code or derivations is not allowed.

Small snippets of publicly available code (e.g., StackOverflow) may be used only if properly attributed, and only when permitted by the homework instructions.


Generative AI tools policy
Unless explicitly permitted by the instructor for a specific assignment, the use of generative AI tools for any part of your homework is not allowed and will be treated as plagiarism.


Regrade policy
Regrade requests are submitted through Gradescope. Include a clear explanation of the grading error.

Deadline: within 1 week of receiving your grade, by 11:59 PM.
We may regrade the entire assignment (your score may go up or down).

Legitimate requests - Points were added incorrectly. - The answer exists but was on a different page. - The grader claims a specific case fails, but your implementation works for that case.
Illegitimate requests - Disagreeing with the rubric / asking for more partial credit without an error. - “My solution wasn’t clear, but what I meant was correct.”

title: Project Guidelines
text: Key dates · Proposal Mar 10 · Progress report Apr 12 · Final report May 12

title: Project Guidelines
text:
Purpose
The course project is your chance to try something new in robotics perception and produce a result that is useful and defensible: a solid reimplementation, a careful evaluation, a meaningful extension, a dataset/toolkit, or a hardware demo with clear analysis.






Where to find project ideas. Start from recent papers at top robotics venues:

ICRA — IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation
IROS — IEEE/RSJ International Conference on Intelligent Robots and Systems
RA-L — IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
T-RO — IEEE Transactions on Robotics

These conferences and journals set the standard for the kind of work we expect — well-scoped problems with clear experimental evaluation. Browsing their proceedings is the best way to find a topic at the right level of depth and rigor.

title: Project Guidelines
text:
Team policy

Projects are done in pairs (2 students).
Solo projects require instructor approval.
Use MS Teams channel:

Project teammate search

Talk to the instructor early if you’re uncertain about scope or feasibility.

title: Project Guidelines
text:
What makes a strong project
A strong project has:

A clear question — what are you trying to learn / verify / build?
A measurable outcome — metrics, ablations, failure cases, qualitative demos.
A baseline and a fair comparison.
Evidence of real work beyond “running someone else’s repo.”

title: Project Guidelines
text:
Acceptable project types
Pick one “flavor”:


Reimplementation + validation
Reproduce a key claim/plot from a recent ICRA/IROS/RA-L/T-RO paper and add one extension.


Code + experimentation
Start from open-source code, but do systematic evaluation, comparisons, and modifications.


Problem-driven system
Design and implement a perception/estimation pipeline with testable outputs.


Dataset / benchmark / toolkit
Include baselines, evaluation scripts, and at least one insight enabled by the dataset.


Hardware-in-the-loop
Implement class methods on real sensors or logged data; document calibration and limitations.








Not sufficient: Running the authors’ code on the authors’ data and reporting their numbers.

title: Project Guidelines
text:
Minimum / Target / Stretch goals
Every team must define three tiers of goals:







Tier
What it means




Minimum (must succeed)
Working pipeline + baseline + dataset + at least one metric


Target
Solid experiments + ablations + clear conclusions + good visuals


Stretch
Ambitious extension if time allows



This framing helps you avoid overcommitment and helps us grade fairly.

title: Project Guidelines
text:
Reproducibility expectations
By the end, your project should be reproducible by a competent student:

Clear README
How to get data / expected folder structure
Dependencies / environment notes
Commands to run
What outputs should look like

title: Project Guidelines
text:
Academic integrity and credit







External code and libraries are allowed with citation.
Your grade reflects your contribution: modifications, experiments, analysis, and code you wrote.
Clearly separate: reused, modified, and written from scratch.

title: Project Guidelines
text:
Grading philosophy
We grade based on project quality and report clarity:







Dimension
What we look for




Technical correctness & depth
Sound formulation, correct implementation


Experimental methodology
Baselines, metrics, fair comparisons


Insight
Failure analysis, interpretation of results


Originality
Idea, extension, evaluation, or dataset choice


Reproducibility & writing
Clean repo, readable report









Negative results can still score well if the exploration is thorough and the analysis is thoughtful.

title: Project Proposal
text: Due: Mar 10 · Length: 2–3 pages (PDF) · Submit via Gradescope (one teammate submits; include both names)

title: Project Proposal
text:
Required contents
Submit a 2–3 page PDF covering the following sections, in order:

1. Team members
Full names and IDs of all team members.


2. Project title
Specific and informative — avoid vague titles like “SLAM Project.”


3. Problem statement & motivation
What are you trying to do, and why does it matter?


4. Approach (high-level plan)

What are the core components / pipeline stages?
Key assumptions or design choices.



5. Evaluation plan

Dataset(s) you will use (include links) or your data collection plan.
Metrics you will report.
Baseline(s) you will compare against.



6. Goals



Tier
Description




Minimum
Brief but concrete


Target
Brief but concrete


Stretch
Brief but concrete





7. Team roles
Who owns which components? How will integration happen?


8. Resources
Compute needs, sensors, libraries, external code you will use, and how you will build on it.


9. Risks & fallback plan
What could go wrong? What will you do if it does?


10. Relationship to your background
What is new vs. familiar? If related to your research / RA work, describe the overlap and the novelty.

title: Project Proposal
text:
Proposal checklist
Use this quick self-check before submitting:

Clear question and scope
Dataset + metrics + baseline identified
Minimum goal is realistic
Risks + Plan B stated
Role split is concrete
